turns.content: Hey everyone, I'm a Java background trying to get my hands on Rust for that systems work. I've been reading about panics but still don't fully understand how catch_unwind actually captures the unwind payload versus just catching the panic message.

Welcome to Rust! That distinction is crucial because catch_unwind doesn't just grab the string; it captures the entire unwind payload including the location and the type of the panic value. However, you need to remember that this only works if your code implements RefUnwindSafe or UnwindSafe bounds depending on what you're trying to do.
Right, so it's not like a try-catch in Java where I can just grab the exception object. What exactly does that payload contain when I use catch_unwind in a test harness?

In a test harness or FFI boundary, you can retrieve the actual panic value along with metadata like location and message if you call resume_unwind on it. But this is tricky because resume_unwind will re-panic if you don't handle it correctly.
So I'm trying to write a test that verifies my panic handling logic, but I keep getting errors about the type not implementing UnwindSafe. Is there a way to force this?

You cannot force it because Rust enforces safety guarantees at the language level. If your code panics with a value that doesn't implement UnwindSafe, catch_unwind will fail to capture it properly and you'll get a panic instead of returning the payload.
That makes sense, but I'm wondering if there's any way to make async code work with this since Rust async is all about safe unwinds.

No, async code cannot rely on catch_unwind because the unwind mechanism in async contexts requires specific safety bounds that are not compatible with how panics propagate through futures. The language design intentionally separates panic handling from async execution paths.
Got it, so I should probably just use resume_unwind and handle the re-panic manually in my test harness? What's the exact pattern for that?

Yes, you can call catch_unwind to get a Result with either Ok or Err, then if it's Err you call resume_unwind on the panic payload to re-panic and continue your test logic. But be careful because this will propagate the original panic context.
Okay, I think I finally get how this works now. It's not just about catching strings but actually capturing the full unwind payload with proper bounds checking.

Exactly! The key insight is that panic::catch_unwind captures the entire unwind state including location and value, while resume_unwind is what allows you to re-enter that panic context. This combination gives you fine-grained control over test harnesses.
